notes.summary: A shadow is the dark area where light is blocked by an object. The size of a shadow depends on the distance between the object and light source, not just the angle of incidence.
notes.importance: Understanding shadows helps in understanding the behavior of light and its interaction with objects.
quick_facts: Shadows can be umbra (dark) or penumbra (lighter)
Umbra is the darker inner shadow region
Penumbra is the lighter outer shadow region

notes.summary: Specular reflection occurs when light hits a mirror at an angle, creating an image behind the mirror. This type of reflection is seen in mirrors and still water.
notes.importance: Understanding specular reflection helps in understanding how images are formed on mirrors and other reflective surfaces.
quick_facts: Light hits the mirror at an angle
Image is formed behind the mirror
Specular reflection occurs due to smooth surface

notes.summary: Refraction occurs when light passes from one medium to another, changing its direction. This phenomenon is responsible for the bending of light around objects.
notes.importance: Understanding refraction helps in understanding how light behaves when it encounters different materials and surfaces.
quick_facts: Light changes direction when passing from one medium to another
Refraction occurs due to change in speed of light
Bending of light around objects is an example of refraction

notes.summary: A concave mirror is a curved mirror that converges light rays. Concave mirrors can produce real, inverted, and enlarged images when objects are placed within the focal length.
notes.importance: Understanding concave mirrors helps in understanding how they can be used to form images and magnify objects.
quick_facts: Images formed by concave mirrors are real, inverted, and enlarged
